overview.body: This is a test lab used to verify that every component type renders correctly. It is not a real assignment.

mentorQuote.quote: If it renders clean, ship it. That's the whole job.

objectives: Verify that every component type renders without errors
Confirm CSS classes are correct end-to-end
Establish a baseline for the full pipeline
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 8: Program 8-3 (string methods). Chapter 9: Programs 9-1 through 9-4 (dictionaries).
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapters 8 & 9
programRequirements: All component types render without errors
overview.saveYourCode: Keep this file — the next lab builds on it.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you're adding this week. The [REPLACED] tags show what changes from your previous code.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
CITY_DISTANCES = { ... }   [NEW — replaces 20+ distance constants]
CITY_INFO      = { ... }   [NEW — city names and taglines]

# ---- HELPER FUNCTIONS ----
display_destinations()     shows menu from dict    [REPLACED]
get_city_info(city)        dict lookup             [NEW]

GAME LOOP:
│
├── Display city info (CITY_INFO lookup)           [REPLACED]
├── Get destination choice
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP:
│   ├── Roll D20, move miles
│   ├── 30% chance: hazard check
│   └── Repeat until arrival
│
└── Update totals, check outcome

content.body: You're replacing 20+ individual distance constants with a single nested dictionary. This is the core data structure upgrade for Module 4.

items: Create a constant CITY_DISTANCES near the top of your file.
Add San Antonio as the first key: CITY_DISTANCES = {'San Antonio': {...}}
Add all five destination cities as nested keys with integer mile values.

references.description: Shows basic dictionary creation with string keys and integer values.

content.output:
Where would you like to travel?
  A) Austin     (80 miles)
  B) Houston    (197 miles)
  C) Dallas     (275 miles)

content.body: One lookup replaces ten branches. This is the moment your code stops being a long if/elif chain and starts being data-driven.

rows: Dictionary keys are case-sensitive — use exact capitalization
Using an if/elif chain instead of dict access
Defeats the purpose of a dictionary; CodeGrade will flag it

content.title: Key Case Matters
content.body: Dictionary keys are case-sensitive. 'austin' and 'Austin' are different keys.

content.title: You're Done When...
content.body: Your program looks up distances using CITY_DISTANCES[city][destination] with no hardcoded numbers in the game loop.

content.title: Test in Isolation
content.body: Before wiring the dictionary into the game, test it by itself.
items: Open a new file and create just the dictionary
Print a few lookups to confirm the values are right
Then copy it into your main file

content.title: Why Nested Dicts?
content.body: A nested dictionary models a two-dimensional lookup: from city → to destination → miles. It's the same concept as a table with row and column headers.

content.title: Scope Violation
content.body: Do not use list comprehensions or try/except — those are Module 4.2 and 4.3 topics. CodeGrade's AST check will flag them.
Run your program and confirm the destination menu shows correct distances pulled from the dictionary.

content.quote: Data structures exist to make the impossible feel obvious. One dictionary lookup instead of twenty constants — that's not just cleaner code, it's a better model of reality.

beforeYouSubmit: lab-test.py uses CITY_DISTANCES — no hardcoded distance constants
All city lookups use dictionary key access, not if/elif chains
Code has top-of-file comment block with name, abc123, section, and description
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: You may use dictionaries, .keys(), .values(), .items(), and string methods from Chapter 8. Do NOT use list comprehensions, try/except, classes, or inheritance.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–9 (Chapters 2–8 fully permitted; Chapter 9 dictionaries as introduced this module)

warnings: Do not use parallel lists in place of a dictionary — CodeGrade's scope check will flag it.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep this file — Module 4.2 adds string method input handling on top of your dictionary work.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Programs 9-1 through 9-4 — dictionary creation, access, and iteration
Test your dictionary in isolation before wiring it into the game
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail
Come to Zoom or office hours — screen share and debug together
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 8 & 9 BookEx Reference Guides — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example
